description: This figure displays engineering stress-strain curves obtained from tensile tests conducted at an initial strain rate of 5×10^-4 s^-1, comparing the mechanical behavior of annealed austenitic Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels with two aluminum contents: 2Al (Fe-30.5Mn-2.1Al-1.2C wt.%) and 8Al (Fe-30.5Mn-8.0Al-1.2C wt.%). Four distinct curves represent different precipitation states: 2Al-450°C/1h (black), 2Al-650°C/72h (red), 8Al-450°C/1h (green), and 8Al-600°C/24h (blue). The 8Al-600°C/24h condition exhibits the highest strength with yield strength of approximately 990 MPa and ultimate tensile strength near 1180 MPa, accompanied by elongation of approximately 37%. The 2Al-450°C/1h sample shows superior ductility with elongation reaching 75% and UTS of about 935 MPa. In contrast, the long-annealed 2Al-650°C/72h displays reduced ductility (approximately 35%) and lower UTS (approximately 850 MPa) due to intergranular M3C carbide precipitation promoting premature fracture. The 8Al-450°C/1h condition demonstrates intermediate properties with UTS of approximately 895 MPa and elongation of 65%. These curves collectively demonstrate that increasing Al content combined with long annealing to precipitate nanosized κ-carbides substantially enhances strength, whereas prolonged annealing of 2Al steel causes embrittlement through coarse intergranular carbide formation.

The figure presents strain hardening rate versus true stress curves for annealed 2Al steels (Fe–30.5Mn–2.1Al–1.2C wt.%) tensile deformed at an initial strain rate of $5 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{s}^{-1}$. Two heat treatment conditions are compared: short annealing at 450°C for 1 h (black curve, labeled 2Al-450°C/1h) and long annealing at 650°C for 72 h (red curve, labeled 2Al-650°C/72h). The short-annealed specimen exhibits a characteristic two-stage hardening behavior, with the first hardening stage at lower stress levels (~400–1000 MPa) followed by a second hardening stage commencing around 1100 MPa. In contrast, the long-annealed sample shows a continuous decrease in strain hardening rate and fractures at a macroscopic true stress of approximately 1150 MPa (indicated by the vertical dashed line), coinciding with the onset of the second hardening regime in the short-annealed alloy. The caption and citation context explain that the long annealing treatment produces coarse intergranular M3C-type carbides along grain boundaries. The figure demonstrates that while deformation twinning activates in both conditions, in the long-annealed steel, twin impingement on these carbides creates local shear stress concentrations that trigger intergranular fracture rather than additional hardening, resulting in limited uniform elongation of approximately 35%.

This figure presents the deformation microstructure and fracture characteristics of long-annealed 2Al steel (Fe-30.5Mn-2.1Al-1.2C wt.%) subjected to tensile loading. Panel (a) is an ECCI (Electron Channeling Contrast Imaging) micrograph showing dislocation cell blocks with an average size of approximately 275 ± 50 nm in a sample strained to 0.2 true strain (scale bar: 500 nm). Panel (b) displays the distribution of coarse intergranular M₃C-type carbides along grain boundaries and annealing twin interfaces, with dimensions of about 1 μm wide by 3 μm long (scale bar: 10 μm). Panel (c) provides an ECCI image capturing the interaction between deformation twin bundles and intergranular M₃C carbides in a sample strained to fracture, illustrating how twin tips impinge upon carbide interfaces (scale bar: 1 μm). Panel (d) shows the surface fracture morphology revealing a clear intergranular fracture mode with faceted grain surfaces (scale bar: 10 μm). The microstructural evidence demonstrates that the long annealing treatment (650°C for 72 h) produces incoherent M₃C carbides at grain boundaries, which interact with deformation twins activated at intermediate-to-high stress levels (estimated twinning stress 670-750 MPa). This twin-carbide interaction generates local shear stress concentrations that trigger intergranular fracture rather than additional strain hardening, explaining the limited uniform elongation (UE = 35%) and fracture at 1150 MPa true stress observed in the long-annealed condition compared to the short-annealed counterpart.

The figure presents strain hardening rate versus true stress curves for an austenitic high-Mn low-density 8Al steel (Fe–30.5Mn–8.0Al–1.2C wt.%) tensile tested at an initial strain rate of $5 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{s}^{-1}}$. Two heat treatment conditions are compared: short annealing at 450°C for 1 h (black curve) and long annealing at 600°C for 24 h (red curve). Both specimens exhibit an initial strain hardening rate of approximately 5 GPa. The short-annealed sample shows a conventional three-stage hardening behavior with a stable plateau around 2 GPa between 800–1100 MPa before declining. In contrast, the long-annealed sample displays a sharp drop in hardening rate to ~2 GPa at ~1150 MPa, followed by a distinct upward inflection to ~2.2 GPa at higher stresses (>1400 MPa). These contrasting behaviors reflect different precipitation states: short annealing produces heterogeneously distributed shearable nanosized κ-carbides (2–5 nm) enabling planar slip and sustained work hardening, whereas long annealing creates a uniform dispersion of cuboidal κ-carbides (10–20 nm) with narrow matrix channels, resulting in higher strength but reduced ductility.

This figure presents the deformation microstructures and precipitation states of annealed 8Al steels (Fe–30.5Mn–8.0Al–1.2C wt.%) under tensile loading, combining ECCI and dark-field TEM imaging. Subfigure (a) shows an ECCI image of Taylor lattice structures in a short-annealed (450 °C/1 h) sample deformed to 0.3 true strain, revealing planar dislocation configurations. Subfigure (b) is a dark-field TEM image displaying the heterogeneous distribution of nanosized κ-carbides (2–5 nm, L'1₂-type) within the short-annealed microstructure, with the inset showing selected-area electron diffraction patterns. Subfigure (c) presents an ECCI image of the long-annealed (600 °C/24 h) sample deformed to 0.15 true strain, where arrows indicate dense planar dislocation arrays spaced 100–300 nm. Subfigure (d) is a dark-field TEM image revealing a uniform distribution of cuboidal κ-carbides (10–20 nm) in the long-annealed condition, with arrows highlighting shearable particles cut by dislocations within narrow matrix channels (~2–3 nm). The figure demonstrates that while short annealing produces fine precipitates promoting moderate strength (YS: 540 MPa), long annealing creates a high volume fraction of cuboidal κ-carbides that stabilize planar slip substructures, resulting in an outstanding strength–ductility combination (YS: 990 MPa, UTS: 1180 MPa, UE: 37%) via the shearing mechanism.
